one_line_takeaway: Agent Audit is an open-source multi-scanner static analyzer purpose-built for LLM agent software (tool-boundary-aware taint analysis, credential detection, and structured MCP-config/privilege checks) that, on the authors' own 42-vulnerability AVB benchmark, achieves 95.24% recall / 86.96% precision (F1=0.909) versus 23.8%/100% (F1=0.385) for Semgrep and 29.7%/100% (F1=0.458) for Bandit, at comparable sub-second scan speed.
key_finding: On the synthetic Layer-1 ground-truth fixtures, the confidence-tiering system achieves 98.51% precision at 100% recall. On the main AVB benchmark (42 oracle vulnerabilities across Set A: injection/RCE, 19 vulns; Set B: MCP/components, 9 vulns; Set C: data/authentication, 14 vulns), Agent Audit achieves 40 true positives / 2 false negatives / 6 false positives -> 95.24% recall, 86.96% precision, F1=0.909. Semgrep achieves 10 TPs / 32 FNs / 0 FPs -> 23.8% recall, 100% precision, F1=0.385. Bandit achieves 11 TPs / 26 FNs / 0 FPs -> 29.7% recall, 100% precision, F1=0.458. This is a 4.0x recall advantage over Semgrep, attributed to three factors: MCP configuration vulnerabilities (10/42 oracle entries, 0% coverage by Semgrep), credential detection (12/42 entries, Semgrep detects only 1), and agent-specific patterns (prompt injection, self-modification, tool poisoning) absent from generic SAST rule sets. 30 of 42 oracle vulnerabilities are exclusively detected by Agent Audit; 0 are exclusive to Semgrep (1 exclusive to Bandit per the table's 'Exclusive detections' row). Per-set recall: Agent Audit 100%/100%/85.7% on Sets A/B/C vs. Semgrep 47.4%/0%/7.1% and Bandit 0%/0%/57.9%. Performance: Agent Audit scans 22,009 lines of Python in 0.87s (~25,000 lines/sec), comparable to Bandit's 0.90s and 6.9x faster than Semgrep's 5.94s on the same codebase; the full test suite (25,582 lines) scans in 1.27s.
core_contribution: The paper presents Agent Audit, an open-source static analysis system (system/tool demo paper, 22,009 LOC / 58 files / 1,323 unit tests) that scans Python LLM-agent code and deployment artifacts (MCP configs) through four parallel scanners -- PythonScanner (AST + intra-procedural taint analysis), SecretScanner (regex + entropy-based credential detection), MCPConfigScanner (structured JSON/YAML parsing of 9 MCP config formats), and PrivilegeScanner (AST/regex privilege-escalation checks) -- feeding a unified RuleEngine of 57 rules mapped to all 10 OWASP Agentic Security Initiative (ASI) categories, with confidence tiering (BLOCK/WARN/INFO/SUPPRESSED) and cross-scanner deduplication. The paper also introduces Agent-Vuln-Bench (AVB), a new 22-sample / 42-annotated-vulnerability benchmark for agent-security scanners, and reports Agent Audit's detection results against Bandit and Semgrep on this benchmark.
threat_model: Software-stack-layer risks in LLM agent deployments, not model-weight attacks: (1) tool functions that route untrusted (user or LLM-generated) input into dangerous operations such as eval(), subprocess.run(), or cursor.execute(); (2) prompt-construction code that interpolates unsanitized user input into system prompts (prompt-injection surfaces); (3) deployment/config artifacts -- MCP server configurations -- that grant over-broad filesystem access, reference unverified third-party MCP servers, or embed exposed credentials; (4) MCP supply-chain risks including cross-server tool shadowing, tool-description poisoning, and argument injection, detected statically (and via a live stdio connection in the `inspect` subcommand) without executing any tools. The tool is a static/pre-deployment auditor, not a runtime defense; it explicitly does not execute or simulate runtime prompt-injection payloads.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: the taint analysis is intra-procedural only -- inter-procedural data flow across function boundaries is not tracked. Python is the primary analysis target; TypeScript/JavaScript receive only regex-level scanning (not AST/taint analysis). Agent Audit detects code-level vulnerability patterns statically but does not execute or simulate runtime prompt-injection payloads, so it cannot confirm exploitability at runtime. Confidence thresholds (BLOCK/WARN/INFO cutoffs) are calibrated empirically rather than derived from a formal model. The two false negatives on AVB Set C are a Markdown-embedded credential (rule AGENT-004) and an unimplemented daemon-detection rule (AGENT-043), both occurring in a TypeScript/Markdown sample outside the tool's primary Python scope -- i.e., a known gap rather than a general detection failure. Additionally, the benchmark (AVB) itself is newly introduced by the same authors evaluating their own tool, with only 22 samples/42 vulnerabilities, and precision differences (86.96% vs. 100% for baselines) partly reflect Agent Audit's much higher recall/coverage rather than baselines being more accurate on comparable ground -- baselines simply flag far fewer categories of issue at all (10 and 11 TPs vs 40).

datasets_used: Agent-Vuln-Bench (AVB) -- new benchmark introduced by the authors: 22 samples, 42 expert-annotated vulnerabilities, split into Set A (injection/RCE, 19 vulns), Set B (MCP/components, 9 vulns), Set C (data/authentication, 14 vulns); includes a KNOWN subset (CVE reproductions, e.g. LangChain LLMMathChain eval() injection CVE-2023-29374, PythonREPLTool RCE CVE-2023-36258) and a WILD subset (production agent code patterns: unsandboxed eval() calculator tools, SSRF via user-controlled URLs in web-fetcher tools, agent self-modification via dynamic importlib usage, plus 3 MCP supply-chain samples: tool shadowing, tool description poisoning, baseline configuration drift)
Synthetic ground-truth fixtures ('Layer 1 evaluation') used to measure the confidence-tiering system in isolation
Agent Audit's own codebase (22,009 lines of Python, 58 source files) and full test suite (25,582 lines) used as the corpus for scan-time/throughput benchmarking
quantitative_results: AVB overall: Agent Audit 40 TP / 2 FN / 6 FP -> 95.24% recall, 86.96% precision, F1=0.909
AVB overall: Semgrep 10 TP / 32 FN / 0 FP -> 23.8% recall, 100.0% precision, F1=0.385
AVB overall: Bandit 11 TP / 26 FN / 0 FP -> 29.7% recall, 100.0% precision, F1=0.458
Exclusive detections: 30/42 vulnerabilities exclusively found by Agent Audit; 0 exclusive to Semgrep
MCP vulnerability coverage: Agent Audit 100%, Semgrep 0%, Bandit 0%
OWASP ASI category coverage: Agent Audit 10/10, Semgrep ~1/10, Bandit ~1/10
Per-set recall -- Set A (injection/RCE): Agent Audit 100%, Semgrep 47.4%, Bandit 0%
Per-set recall -- Set B (MCP/components): Agent Audit 100%, Semgrep 0%, Bandit 0%
Per-set recall -- Set C (data/authentication): Agent Audit 85.7%, Semgrep 7.1%, Bandit 57.9%
Layer-1 synthetic fixture evaluation: 98.51% precision at 100% recall
Scan speed: Agent Audit 0.87s for 22,009 lines (~25,000 lines/sec) vs Bandit 0.90s vs Semgrep 5.94s (Agent Audit 6.9x faster than Semgrep); full 25,582-line test suite scans in 1.27s
System scale: 22,009 LOC, 58 source files, 1,323 unit tests, 57 detection rules mapped from 73 pattern types, >20 distinct false-positive-reduction mechanisms
Confidence tiers: BLOCK >=0.92, WARN >=0.60, INFO >=0.30, SUPPRESSED <0.30; tool-boundary base confidence 0.90 vs 0.55 for non-tool functions; sanitizer detection multiplies confidence by 0.20
